coredata.dc:title: OCV Estimation in electrochemical batteries with an unknown linear model and finite excitation

coredata.dc:description: This study introduces a novel observer architecture for estimating the model parameters and the Open Circuit Voltage (from which State of Charge can be obtained) in electrochemical systems that can be modeled through an equivalent circuit, like lithium-ion and vanadium redox-flow batteries. The proposed methodology leverages the recently proposed Generalized Parameter Estimation Based Observer framework to transform the state estimation problem into a parameter estimation problem. This novel approach can be exploited to relax the Persistence of Excitation requirement, enabling an accurate estimation in a wider variety of scenarios. Numerical simulations across various current profiles, including one that guarantees persistent excitation and real-world driving cycles, validate the robustness of the estimator under both noiseless and noisy conditions. Experimental results from lithium-ion and vanadium redox-flow battery setups further corroborate the accuracy of the architecture, demonstrating its capability to estimate Open Circuit Voltage and reconstruct terminal voltage effectively.

Vanadium redox-flow batteries
SoC estimation
Parameter identification
Relaxation of the persistence of excitation condition

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study introduces a novel observer architecture for estimating the model parameters and the Open Circuit Voltage (from which State of Charge can be obtained) in electrochemical systems that can be modeled through an equivalent circuit, like lithium-ion and vanadium redox-flow batteries. The proposed methodology leverages the recently proposed Generalized Parameter Estimation Based Observer framework to transform the state estimation problem into a parameter estimation problem. This novel approach can be exploited to relax the Persistence of Excitation requirement, enabling an accurate estimation in a wider variety of scenarios. Numerical simulations across various current profiles, including one that guarantees persistent excitation and real-world driving cycles, validate the robustness of the estimator under both noiseless and noisy conditions. Experimental results from lithium-ion and vanadium redox-flow battery setups further corroborate the accuracy of the architecture, demonstrating its capability to estimate Open Circuit Voltage and reconstruct terminal voltage effectively.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Relaxation of the persistence of excitation condition

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Electrochemical systems are the predominant solution for energy storage in small and medium-sized applications [1], due to their diverse types offering a wide range of characteristics. These properties make them suitable for various applications, including consumer electronics, transportation, and ancillary services for the electrical grid.

Although the advances of this work may be applied to many electrochemical storage system, the focus will be on two particular types. First, Lithium-Ion Batteries (LIB) due to their widespread use in electric vehicle applications and their increasing integration into electrical grids [2]. Second, Vanadium Redox Flow Batteries (VRFB) are considered [3,4] due to their unique properties that facilitate the validation of our proposal. The specific characteristics of VRFB are discussed in Section 5.2.

A deeper understanding of the behavior of electrochemical systems is crucial for optimizing and controlling their performance. Two commonly evaluated metrics are the State of Charge (SoC), representing the energy stored in the system, and the State of Health (SoH), quantifying system degradation. Knowledge of these metrics, along with some additional parameters, is essential for the effective and safe operation of any electrochemical system. However, these metrics are often not directly measurable due to engineering and economic factors, which limit the number of sensors on these systems. This limitation motivates the development of algorithms that provide an estimation of these indices.

For SoC estimation, several methods are available, each one with advantages and disadvantages. One approach involves measuring the Open Circuit Voltage (OCV), which is directly related to SoC [5] through a static and (usually) injective function. However, this method is only valid when the battery is at a rest state (i.e., the current has been zero for a long enough time interval). During active operation, the measured voltage deviates from the OCV, as explained in Section 2.1.

Another common technique is Coulomb counting [6], which integrates the current entering or leaving the battery over time to estimate its accumulated charge. This method, however, requires knowledge of the initial SoC and the system’s nominal capacity. In practice, two sources of error can significantly affect its accuracy. First, current measurement errors — though small — accumulate over time, leading to drift in the SoC estimate. Second, the effective capacity of the battery can vary depending on degradation or the operating conditions, such as temperature and current rate. In particular, high current amplitudes cause increased voltage drops due to internal resistance, which can prematurely drive the terminal voltage below the cutoff threshold, effectively reducing the usable capacity. As a result, Coulomb counting may significantly overestimate the SoC under such conditions. Additional limitations of this method are discussed in [6].

An alternative to direct SoC measurement, is to estimate these indicators through algorithms based on easy to measure signals. These estimation techniques rely on mathematical models of the battery, which can be broadly categorized into three families, with decreasing precision and computational and parametrization cost:

ce:list-item.ce:para: Mechanistic models [7], which offer detailed descriptions of the physical phenomena within the battery. While highly accurate, these models are computationally expensive and challenging to parameterize, making them more suitable for cell design.

Data-driven models [8,9], which leverage machine learning techniques to predict battery behavior. These models establish relationships between measurable variables and desired parameters or estimations. Their performance depends heavily on the quality, quantity and relevance of training data. Without sufficient data, extensive experimentation is required for training.

Equivalent Circuit Model (ECM), approximate battery behavior using electrical circuits [10], and sometimes incorporate fractional models [11]. ECMs strike a balance between accuracy and computational complexity, making them interpretable, computationally efficient, and suitable for applications requiring moderate accuracy. Moreover, similar ECM can be used in different electrochemical batteries.

ce:section.ce:para: For the mentioned advantages of ECM, such model is employed in this study, particularly a 1st-order model (see Section 2.1). This approach is not unique, as ECM are a widespread approach modeling electrochemical systems. Examples can be found in [10,12–14] for LIBs and [3,4,15] for VRFB.

Estimation of SoC and SoH, especially with mechanistic models and ECM, often requires observers. That is, algorithms that iteratively compute the estimations based on measured data. They can be classified between linear and nonlinear observers. In our previous work [16], we reviewed observers applied to LIBs. Based on model linearization, the most popular observer technique is by far the Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) and its many variations. Since being first applied in LIBs in [17], many articles have used it, modified it or combined with other techniques for estimation of SoC or SoH purposes [18–21].

As many battery models are intrinsically nonlinear, nonlinear observers may provide better performance in terms of transient behavior and model robustness. In this context, the Unscented Kalman Filter (UKF) has also received important attention, as can be seen in [22–24]. Sliding Mode Observer (SMO) is also quite a popular technique, as they can guarantee robust convergence of the states when certain observability properties are met [25–27]. Finally, we mention the High Gain Observer (HGO) [28,29], that reduces the commutations inherent to SMO and also presents robust convergence properties.

The above references focus on estimating SoC directly. However, our approach will consist in estimating first OCV, as both magnitudes are directly related. In the literature, some works that uses this workflow are the following: [13,30,31]. All of them use ECM and different estimation methods to obtain OCV. In the case of [13], a least squares algorithm has been used to simultaneously estimate the states of the system and the circuit parameters. In [31], a variation of an EKF has been used with the same goal, while finally in [30] the OCV is not estimated by itself but is part of the equations that allow to estimate SoC. However, these estimators still face the same limitations as the other SoC estimators.

However, if the parameters of the models are unknown, or vary, the performance of the observer is compromised. Indeed, ECMs are usually calibrated on a range of operating points. Consequently, these types of model are very sensitive to the change of the battery operating conditions. To provide a robust solution that can work with unknown parameters and unknown battery operating regions, adaptive observers try to estimate both parameters and states at the same time. [32] uses a Lyapunov-based adaptation law for and ECM with this purpose, and [33] employs for the same reasons a nonlinear variation of the EKF. However, both approaches face the same limitation: they strongly depend on the richness of the available measured data for the states and parameters to converge to its real values. PE is a well-known condition (discussed here in Section 3.5) that ensures this richness over time. However, this stringent requirement can be relaxed to Interval Excitation (IE), a less strict condition, using the recently proposed observer architecture described in [34], which forms the basis of our approach.

With all the mentioned above, we find that the problem of estimating OCV (or SoC) is still unresolved, specially in the case of unknown model parameters, but the current methods do not thoroughly consider the conditions in which the estimation can be achieved. Therefore, in this paper, we propose an observer architecture that relaxes the PE condition to IE and applies it to estimate the OCV of a battery using an ECM with unknown parameters. The main contributions of this work are:

ce:list-item.ce:para: Development of transformations to adapt the 1st-order ECM for parameter and OCV estimation without prior system knowledge.

Application of a novel observer architecture from [34], marking its first application to LIBs and VRFB, to the best of the authors’ knowledge. This architecture demonstrates the relaxation of the PE condition to IE.
Implementation of a Plug&Play estimation methodology, requiring only the OCV-SoC relationship for SoC estimation.
Experimental validation of the proposed architecture in LIBs and VRFB.

ce:section.ce:para: The paper is organized as follows: Section 2 outlines the LIB model, estimation objectives, and the general idea of the observer architecture. In Section 3, the model from Section 2 is transformed into a state-space representation, and all the transformations and observer architecture are described. Section 4 describes the simulation profiles, models, and results. Finally, Section 5 explains the experimental setup, introduces VRFB, and compares results using a conventional KF.

There exist a wide variety of ECM, with increasing complexity often assumed to improve precision as more elements are added to the model. While greater complexity can better capture the model’s behavior, it also increases the computational cost of observer solutions. In [10], a comparison of different ECM was made for different LiB chemistries, finding that even a simple model such as the 1st order ECM exhibits sufficiently small RMSE compared to measured data, making it suitable for most estimation problems. Thus, in this work a 1st-order ECM is considered. A scheme of the considered electrical circuit is presented in Fig. 1.

The function f of Eq. (3) is injective. This yields the possibility of computing SoC by estimating the OCV first, as seen in Eq. (3).

ce:section.ce:para:
With this in mind, the estimation objective (4) can be substituted by the objective of generating an estimation of the unmeasured
u
O C V
. If such task is achieved, an accurate estimation of the SoC can be achieved by inverting the function f as presented in (3).

Before presenting the main observer design, it is convenient to transform the model (1)–(2) into a state-space representation (Section 3.1). Such representation will clarify two properties of the considered problem. On the one hand, if the parameter τ is known, then the system can be transformed into a Linearly Parametrized Problem (LPP). Consequently, the estimation problem can be addressed with multiple strategies, such as the Kalman filter or the Generalized Parameter Estimation-Based Observer (GPEBO) technique [39] (which will be explored in Section 3.2). Nevertheless, in practice, the parameter τ is unknown. The second property is that a filtering structure, detailed in Section 3.3, allows to transform the model in (1) and (2) into a LPP where the unknown parameter τ enters linearly. This LPP allows the estimation of the unknown parameter τ with standard gradient descent or least squares algorithms [40]. This work proposes estimating τ using the technique from [34], detailed in Section 3.4. Considering these properties, the proposed algorithm will consist in the cascade interconnection of two estimators, one that estimates the unknown parameter τ and a second one that, based on this first estimation, estimates the rest of parameters and states. This procedure is detailed in Fig. 2. The remainder of this section is dedicated to detailing this architecture.

To ease the observer design process, it is convenient to rewrite (1)–(2) into a state-space form, where we embed the unknown parameters

Noticeably, if the parameter τ is known, the battery model can be represented as a LTV system. Section 3.2, we will show that, the state estimation problem (5) can be transformed into a parameter estimation problem with a LPP structure using the technique presented in [39]. These new parameters can be estimated through the algorithm presented in Section 3.4. Then, in Section 3.3, we will show that a second regression problem can be obtained to estimate the parameter τ .

We recall again that the Φ dynamics in (7) depend on the unknown parameter τ . Thus, the vector Ψ cannot be directly computed if τ is unknown. Nonetheless, in the next section we will show how to obtain a second LPP that can be used to estimate the unknown parameter τ .

Sections 3.2 and 3.3 have constructed two linear regressions that can be used to solve the considered estimation problem. In the literature, there are multiple real-time algorithms that can be used to estimate the unknown parameters from a linear regression, see [40] for a recent review. Typically, these algorithms produce a reliable estimation by minimizing observed errors using methods such as gradient descent [42], least squares [43], high-order sliding mode differentiators [44], or neural networks [45]. However, their accuracy and robustness rely on the assumption of persistence of excitation [40]. This means that the system’s inputs must persistently influence its internal states so that parameter effects are detectable in the measured signals. While it is evident that these signals must contain sufficient information to estimate unknown parameters, what is less obvious is that this excitation must be maintained over time. If, at any point during the battery operation, the excitation condition is not met, real-time parameter estimation degrades, potentially leading to unreliable results and numerical instability. Additionally, battery systems typically require minimal input variations to prevent degradation, making persistent excitation difficult to achieve and, in some cases, undesirable. This fundamental limitation hinders the practical application of such algorithms in battery systems and, to the authors’ knowledge, has been largely overlooked in SoC estimation research.

Considering this, next section studies the use of the estimator proposed in [34] in the proposed linear regressions. This algorithm presents provable parameter estimation under an interval excitation assumption, which is a much weaker requirement than persistence of excitation.

In this work, we propose using the Least Squares (LS)-Dynamic Regressor Extension and Mixing (DREM) method introduced in [34]. More precisely, for a LPP of the form

An in-depth explanation of the architecture can be found in [46, Remark 3]. In the remainder if this section, we will provide a brief overview.

We highlight that, under an excitation condition known as interval of excitation (more details are provided in Section 3.5), the scalar factor
Δ
( t )
can be guaranteed to be strictly positive and bounded. This property is sufficient for (17) to generate a consistent estimation of the unknown parameters θ . A more intuitive interpretation of this process is that the first estimator in (15) accumulates and stores information over time until the signals become sufficiently rich. Once this condition is met, the second estimator begins generating a consistent estimation of the parameters.

The dynamics of z in (18) serve a dual purpose. First, z acts as a dynamic scaling factor that bounds Δ , depending on the forgetting factor β . Second, it introduces a decay mechanism that ensures Δ remains sensitive to changes in the system dynamics. This two-estimator architecture is thoroughly discussed in [34,46].

We highlight that this architecture is based on the idea of dynamic regressor extension and mixing (DREM). A recently proposed modification of the more classical parameter estimation algorithms [47,48], which have been proven to improve the transient performance of the estimator while having provable convergence properties under weak observability conditions.

To conclude, we remark that the application of the architecture to the two LPP Eqs. (9), (12) obtained is the following. For the estimation of τ , the LPP in (12) is used in the LS-DREM architecture presented in this section. The third element of the estimated parameter vector
θ
ˆ
corresponds to the estimated value of τ , and is then introduced in (7) in order to compute Ψ of (9). Then, the LPP in (9) is introduced in the DREM architecture of this section to obtain an estimation of the error of the initial conditions of the parameters (parameters θ in (8)), which will lead to the reconstruction of the states through (8).

A consistent estimation of the unknown parameters θ in a linear regression of the form (14) requires the measurable signals y and ϕ to contain sufficient information of the unknown parameters, that is, the system needs to be observable. Common parameter estimators require a very stringent observability assumption known as Persistence of Excitation (PE). The architecture considered in 3.4 is designed to relax the stringent requirements of PE by instead relying on the weaker condition known as Interval of Excitation (IE). First, we define PE and IE.

PE is a classical condition for parameter convergence in solving the linearly parametrized estimation of parameters. For a bounded regressor signal
ϕ
( t )
∈
R
q
, PE is defined as the existence of some positive constants
α > 0
and
T > 0
such that:
(22)
∫
t
t + T
ϕ
( τ )
ϕ
⊤
( τ )
d τ ≥ α
I
q
,
∀ t ≥ 0 .
This condition requires the regressor ϕ to be sufficiently rich over all time intervals of length T , providing persistent information about the parameters to be estimated. While PE ensures global exponential convergence of the parameter estimation error, it is often unrealistic in some practical scenarios.

IE is a weaker condition than PE as it allows for periods of insufficient excitation, requiring only that the regressor exhibits sufficient richness over certain intervals. Mathematically, it is expressed as the existence of some positive constants
α > 0
and
T > 0
such that
(23)
∫
0
T

( τ )

⊤
( τ )
d τ ≥ α
I
q
.
The definition is similar to the one of PE in (22), but just considering a fixed window between from 0 to T , thus comprising the totality of time. A graphical interpretation can be found in the first plot of Fig. 7, where the first 50 s would ensure PE, but the whole cycle can only guarantee IE. The IE condition is sufficient to guarantee asymptotic convergence of the parameter estimation error under the architecture proposed in Section 3.4.

The cascaded estimator architecture allows, as mentioned, to have one estimator gathering information until it is rich enough to activate the second estimator. This design allows to transform the multi-dimensional regression problem into scalar, decoupled regression equations. As is proved in [34], if the original regression is IE, then Δ is not square-integrable and therefore PE.
To test the proposed architecture, numerical simulations have been conducted. The simulated cases are as follows:

ce:list-item.ce:para: PE profile: a current profile composed by a combination of three sine functions is introduced in the model to guarantee that (5) ensures PE.

IE profile: a current profile composed by a combination of two sine functions during the first half of the simulation and a constant value of current during the second half. This current profile makes the system (5) IE.

Worldwide Harmonized Light Vehicles Test Procedure (WLTP) driving cycle [49]: the standardized driving cycle for vehicle homologation in the European Union. The cycle gives data about vehicle velocity and time, and thus it is possible to transform it into power. In turn, this power can be scaled to a level of current realistic to the simulated cell.

ce:section.ce:para: For these scenarios, we will study the noiseless case and the case where an additive Gaussian noise is added in the voltage measurement. In general, the initial SoC of the battery is set to 90% if a different value is not mentioned. However, after checking the viability of the proposal with an ideal case, it is important to discuss the model used for simulation before focusing on the results. The performance of each simulation can be seen in The tuning parameters of the estimator used in each simulation can be found in Appendix B.

The first of the simulations aims to check the validity of our proposal. Thus, a profile ensuring PE is required, and in this case has been a combination of 3 sine functions of the following characteristics: 5 A of amplitude at 10 rad/s, 3 A at
2
2
rad/s and 4 A at
10
3
rad/s. The parameters of the ECM are fixed to the arbitrary values of
R
0
= 0 . 03 Ω
,
R
1
= 0 . 15 Ω
and
C
1
= 5 F
. The OCV has been set constant at 3.3 V. In Fig. 3 we show this case without noise, showing how effectively the estimation of τ and OCV is achieved with precision. It can be seen how the estimate is reached with little error. In the figure on the top, the current profile composed of three sines is depicted. In the second figure, the estimated value of τ is depicted. In the third figure, the OCV estimate can be seen, with a minimal RMSE of 0.002 mV, see Table 4 for comparison. Finally, in the bottom figure the reconstructed terminal voltage can be seen. All plots show the value in the steady-state with a detail depicting the transient. The rest of the parameters are not shown but converge as well to the described values. The gains of the observers can be found in Table 7 for the τ estimator and in Table 9 for the parameter and estates estimator.

For the same test, with noise of a variance of 0.005, the result performs without noticeable deviations. Fig. 4 shows its results. Again, the estimations of τ and OCV achieve a consistent value, with acceptable error, thus proving the robustness of the proposed architecture. The error in the estimation of τ is different in this case, with a greater but still acceptable error. The value of OCV varies but remains in an error of less than 1 mV. For the case of the reconstructed voltage, it can be seen how it neglects the noisy peaks and the sine variation is reproduced with accuracy. The RMSE of the estimate is of 0.41 mV, see Table 4 for comparison. The gains of the observers can be found in Table 7 for the τ estimator and in Table 9 for the parameter and states estimator.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Details of the realistic simulation model
ce:section.ce:para:
The previous section has validated the proposal in an ideal case. In the following section, we will validate it in a more realistic simulations. More precisely, we have recreated a synthetic battery following again the model described in (1), (2). The parameters
R
0
,
R
1
and
C
1
have values similar to those in [10], where the ECM parameters are obtained for several cells, including a Lithium-Ferro-Phosphate (LFP) cell. The values used for simulation are in Table 2. For the values of
u
O C V
, we obtained an experimental relation between OCV and SoC, as explained in Section 4.3.

The procedure used for the simulation is depicted in Fig. 5. With the measurement of the current, we obtain the OCV by means of a Coulomb counter. The current and the OCV, together with the parameters of the ECM from [10] generate the terminal voltage. With the measurements of voltage and current, the observer architecture reconstructs the parameters and the OCV of the battery.

This work uses a OCV-SoC curve that has been obtained experimentally. To obtain this relation, a common experiment consists in a slow discharge at 0.05 C. C-rate is the amplitude of the current related to the capacity, such that 1C will fully charge or discharge a battery in 1 h. Starting from the maximum charge, the cell was discharged until the minimum voltage allowed according to data sheet [50] was reached, and then, charged at the same current amplitude. The average between the two curves will correspond to OCV. The reason behind this low current amplitudes is that the effect of the current in the deviation between the measured voltage and OCV is reasonably low (see Eq. (2)). This is a well known procedure to obtain the OCV-SoC relation, and is described in [14] as well as used in [10], from which we obtained the ECM parameters. With the data from the OCV experimental curve, the computation of SoC can be done with some assumptions.

ce:list-item.ce:para: The initial SoC is known: following the charge procedure indicated in the data sheet, we can know when the cell is fully charged.
The mentioned experiment also allows to obtain an approximation of value of the cell capacity.
The OCV-SoC relation does not suffer from variations in the ambient temperature.

ce:section.ce:para: With the OCV-SoC relation, and input as a look-up table, by integrating the current we can compute the OCV, and then compare it with the estimated value.
We provide a plot of the look-up table showing the relation between OCV and SoC in Fig. 6.

In the first scenario we assume that the voltage measurement
u
b a t
is free of any noise. We show that the proposed algorithm achieves a consistent estimation of the OCV. As the other parameters are less significant, for the sake of readability and compactness are not shown here. We remind the reader that the initial SoC for all the test is set to 90%. Due to the flat SoC-OCV curve of LFP batteries, it can be seen how the OCV curve is practically constant in short profiles.

For the case of a current profile ensuring only IE, Fig. 7 depicts the results, again without noise. The profile has been created combining two sine functions of 2 A of amplitude and a frequency of 10 rad/s and 3 A at 5 rad/s. The reason behind the change of profile between the case ensuring PE and this case is creating a current profile that can be replicated later in experimentation. In the figure on the top, the current profile is depicted. Note how from the instant
t = 50
s
forward, the excitation is completely lost. On the middle figure, the OCV estimate can be seen, again with a minimal error. In the third figure, it can be seen how the voltage starts decreasing as result of the discharge of the battery. The RMSE of the estimate is of 2.19 mV, see Table 4 for comparison. The gains of the observers can be found in Table 8 for both the τ estimator and the parameter and estates estimator.

As it can be seen, in this “ideal” case with synthetic current profiles, the estimation architecture performs adequately, without any inconvenience due to the loss of excitation. Regarding a more realistic profile, in Fig. 8, the case of the WLTP driving cycle can be seen. The simulation time extends now to 1800 s. On the top plot the current corresponding to the WLTP driving cycle scaled to the cell. In the middle figure, it can be seen how after a comparatively longer transient (about 25 s), the OCV estimate is accurate and does not get affected by the loss of excitation in the areas where the current is 0. The greatest variation happens at the end of the cycle due to large peaks of current. Again, on the bottom figure, the reconstructed terminal voltage with little error. The RMSE of the estimate is of 17.80 mV, see Table 4 for comparison. The gains of the observers can be found in Table 10 for the τ estimator, and in Table 9 for the parameter and estates estimator.

For this case only, as it is the most realistic scenario in the simulation environment, but with known ECM parameters, the estimated values of the model are presented in Fig. 9. It can be seen how the parameters converge with an acceptable precision, in the same way as the estimation of OCV shown above. On the top plot the value of τ is depicted, while on the middle the value of
C
1
is shown. Finally, on the bottom plot, the value of
R
0
can be seen. The value of
R
1
can be obtained using the definition of τ .

In this second scenario, we will study the effects of additive Gaussian noise in the voltage measurement. The results of the profile guaranteeing IE are shown in Fig. 10. In this case, the noise had a variance of 0.005. This means that the voltage measurement will have a maximum probable variation of 0.21 V, an error in the line of the cheapest commercial voltmeter. However, this adverse situation produces an acceptable enough estimate. In the middle plot, once the excitation is lost, the OCV estimate stabilizes to a value close to the computed one. In the bottom plot, again, the effect of the noise is neglected by the terminal voltage reconstruction. The RMSE of the estimate is of 20.57 mV, see Table 4 for comparison. The gains of the observers can be found in Table 8 for both the τ estimator and the parameter and estates estimator.

Finally, the test of the WLTP driving cycle with noise is presented in Fig. 11. In this case, the noise had a variance of 0.001. Comparing this simulation against the noiseless case (Fig. 8), it can be seen in the middle figure that the noise affects mainly in the transient, but once the steady-state is reached, the difference is minimal. Again, the greatest error is found in the finishing part of the cycle, where the big current amplitudes greatly affect the terminal voltage and the estimation of the parameters suffers from it. The same way the other noisy cases, the reconstructed voltage in the bottom figure rejects the effect of the added noise. The RMSE of the estimate is of 23.20 mV, see Table 4 for comparison. The gains of the observers can be found in Table 10 for the τ estimator, and in Table 9 for the parameter and estates estimator.

It can be concluded that the proposed estimation architecture successfully estimates the modeled parameters, including OCV and is also successful in reconstructing the terminal voltage, even in the presence of a notable noise in the sensor. To close the section, mention that in both cases the values of the ECM parameters and τ reach consistent values, affected by noise in a similar manner as OCV, but acceptably close to the modeled ones. Of further interest is, the experimental validation of the architecture.

In this section we will validate experimentally the proposed algorithm in two electrochemical batteries of different nature, but, that can be modeled through and equivalent circuit of the form 1. These batteries are a LIB cell and a VRFB. In this section, a description of the setup for both LIB and VRFB is given, together with a brief explanation of VRFB, as well as some insight on how the results are produced. Finally, the result of the two experiments performed is discussed. The tuning parameters of the estimator used in each experimental validation can be found in Appendix B.

Whereas the setup for each battery technology is different, the testing machine and procedures are the same. The test are conducted with a battery testing machine of a company named Neware, the BTS4000-5V12 A, which is responsible for acting as load/source as well as data acquisition system, measuring in a four-wire setup current and voltage. The machine also includes a software that allows the programming of custom charge/discharge profiles and the storage of the measured data. A simple depiction of the operation can be seen in Fig. 12.

Some relevant data about the testing machine are given in Table 3. For the case of the experimentation with the LIB, the cell is simply connected to the BTS4000 as shown in Fig. 12. Two photographs of the setup are shown on Fig. 13. The used LIB cell is a commercial 3200 mAh LFP in a cylindrical 26650 format, and the data sheet can be found in [50]. For the case of the VRFB, details are given in next section.

A diagram of a VRFB appears in Fig. 14. The main characteristic of this technology is that power and energy can be decoupled, as the power is proportional to the surface of the membrane of the cell, and the energy depends on the capacity of the tank that stores the liquid acid solution that is in charge of storing the electrochemical energy. This acid solution is a dissolution of vanadium and its several species in sulfuric acid. We cite the reviews [3,4] for more insights in VRFB.

The liquid solution needs to be pumped into the cell, and this flow reacting through a membrane produces a voltage, which will depend on the concentration of the reactive (related to SoC). This situation, with no load connected, allows to directly measure the OCV. This property of VRFB makes it very attractive for validating the algorithm, since most battery systems cannot directly measure its OCV. In our laboratory setup, we have two identical cells, but only one is connected to the load. As they are fed with the same reactive, we can simultaneously measure the battery voltage under normal operation and the OCV.
As mentioned, the testing machine is the same for both batteries, but in the case of the VRFB, the setup is more complex. It can be seen in Fig. 15.

Besides the BTS4000 testing machine (element 7), paying attention to Fig. 15 one can find all the elements necessary in a VRFB. In elements 1 & 2, we find the tanks that store the different solutions of the electrolyte, connected to the cells (3 & 4) by means of the peristaltic pump (5). Finally, the cells are connected to the battery tester through clamps (element 6), in a four-wire connection where a pair of clamps measures while the other bears the current. The fact that there are two cells being fed the same electrolyte is crucial to measure OCV, as we can run the desired current profile through one of the cells and use the other just for voltage measurement.

The main characteristic of lithium-ion batteries is their ability to store and deliver energy efficiently through reversible electrochemical reactions. These batteries use lithium ions dissolved in a typically liquid electrolyte, facilitating ion transport between the anode and cathode during charge and discharge cycles. The electrolyte allows lithium ions to move between the electrodes, and this migration through a separator generates a potential difference, which is related to the concentration of lithium in the electrodes. In contrast with VRFB, the system here is closed, and thus power and energy are coupled. OCV cannot be directly measured either, unless there is no charge connected and the battery is relaxed. We refer to the review [16] for further insights.

The procedure used to validate the proposed architecture using the gathered data is as follows. In both VRFB and LIB we have measurement of terminal voltage and current. However, for the case of VRFB we only need to compare the measured OCV with the estimated using the proposed architecture. For the LIB, the validation needs to obtain SoC using a Coulomb counter, and then compute the OCV as a function of SoC through a look-up table, as explained in Section 4.3.

In order to compare the performance of the estimation architecture with a more common estimator, an EKF is proposed, due to its widespread popularity. The main difference between our proposal and the EKF is that, in the face of a nonlinearity in the model (the product between the parameter τ and the states in (5)), the EKF applies a linearization to approximate the nonlinear model by a linear one. We remark that such approximation will only be adequate if the states of the linear model are sufficiently close to the original nonlinear one. Consequently, the convergence of the EKF requires an initialization “close enough” to the true value, a process that may be very complex in practice. We highlight that this limitation also happens in other modifications of the EKF which are also based on a linearization, e.g the dual EKF [52]. Our proposal is not based on any system linearization, and thus overcomes this initialization issue.

Although an adequate tuning of the parameters of the EKF may alleviate this inconvenience, EKF will not to provide a fair comparison. Therefore, we propose a KF for the model in (5), which assumes τ as a known parameter. However, as the ECM parameters are unknown in the experimental case, the value used is the τ estimate by the first DREM observer. As the model is linear, the simple Kalman filter is appropriate. The equations used to describe the basic Kalman Filter are the following [53]:
(24)
K = P

is the state covariance matrix, R is the measurement covariance matrix and Q is the process noise covariance matrix. However, as inherent to any KF variations of any kind, as well as most observers, the requirement of PE is still an issue. We need to highlight the fact that here the Kalman filter is implemented with a known value of τ , while our proposal is implemented with an unknown τ .

In all the following figures, the results of the proposed architecture are compared against the simple KF, and moreover, in Fig. 17, the comparison with the EKF is also included to exemplify the initialization problem. The WLTP validation has been chosen because is the case in which the simple KF performs best. However, in the detail of the transient of OCV, it can be seen how the EKF is unable to estimate OCV, and remains far away of the other estimators. In this case, the estimation of terminal voltage by EKF is omitted to allow the visualization of the other estimators. Let it be mentioned that EKF reconstructs the terminal voltage as well as the KF.
To validate the proposed architecture, the following experiments have been carried out:

ce:list-item.ce:para: A simple current profile guaranteeing only IE over the VRFB.
The WLTP driving cycle over the LIB cell.
The IE-only profile across all the LIB SoC .

ce:section.ce:para:
The first experiment conducted is aimed to validate the proposed estimation architecture in the simplest way and can be seen in Fig. 16. To do so, the profile ensuring IE has been applied to the VRFB, as can be seen in the top figure, where the current profile guarantees PE only until
t = 50
s
. Then, in the middle figure, the estimation of the OCV provided by the proposed architecture is directly compared with the OCV measurements in the battery. Moreover, the same data is introduced into a Kalman filter for comparison. In the detail, the transient can be appreciated, as well as the failure of Kalman filter, which remains far away from the target. Focusing on OCV, it can be seen how the estimation against the measure performs greatly while the excitation holds, only to slightly drift away once it is lost. On the other hand, the Kalman filter is unable to reach a close value, as can be seen in the detail of the figure. The RMSE of the estimate is of 2.71 mV for the DREM-LS against the 169.90 mV of the KF, see Table 4 for comparison. Finally, on the bottom figure, the reconstructed battery voltage can be seen. The gains of the observers can be found in Table 8 for the both estimators.

The case of the WLTP driving cycle has also been validated, after a proper escalation adequate to the characteristics of the cell used. Its results can be seen in Fig. 17. In this case, the experiment was initiated at 100% SoC. In the middle plot it can be seen how the proposed architecture converges faster in terms of OCV than the Kalman filter and also performs closer to the computed point, oscillating less than the KF. Let it be reminded that the Kalman filter knows beforehand the value of τ . In this Figure, we find also the comparison with the EKF, which ultimately fails in estimating OCV. In the zoomed section, it can be seen that the proposed architecture converges faster than the Kalman filter. Moreover, the failed attempt of the EKF is also displayed. The RMSE of the estimate is of 37.86 mV for the DREM-LS against the 70.10 mV of the KF or the 161.06 mV of the EKF, see Table 4 for comparison. Finally, in the bottom plot, the reconstruction of the terminal voltage is shown. Although not plotted, the estimation of the parameters of the ECM by the proposed architecture reaches values in the range of the simulated ones. The gains of the observers can be found in Table 10 for the τ observer and in Table 9 for the parameter and states observer.

The next experiment is a sweep across all the SoC values of the LIB, where the IE profile seen in the previous experiments is repeated at various SoC intervals. This result can be seen in Fig. 18, where the current profile (top figure) shows the IE profile followed by a constant discharge (for the first half of the cycle) and charge (for the second half). This procedure ensures that the IE profile is tested during all SoC ranges. In the bottom Figure, we only show the estimated OCV. The computed case, in blue, serves as benchmark, as it comes from the Coulomb counter. The red line corresponds to the usual architecture used throughout the paper. However, the reader will notice that once the battery is discharged (seconds 1e4 to 1.5e4), the estimation of OCV greatly diverges from the original value. When the charge begins, the usual architecture fails to track the changes. The reason for this is that the observer loses its alertness, or mathematically, as the Δ in Eq. (20) converges to 1, the estimated parameters also converge to the real ones and the adaptive gain (16) converges to 0. As the parameters, particularly OCV, change as a result of the discharge, the observer is unable to follow these changes.

This situation motivates the creation of a reset architecture, based on the one described in [34], and its performance can be seen in the yellow line in Fig. 18. Let it be mentioned here that this reset only affects the second part of the estimation architecture and does not affect the estimation of the τ element of the model (keep in mind Fig. 2). When the reset conditions are met, the Δ value is set to 0 and thus the observer regains its alertness. Again, although not shown in the plot, the estimated parameters of the ECM by our proposal fall within the range of the simulated values.

In this case in particular, it is important to mention that the reset produce a situation where no steady state is definitely reached, as the estimator has to converge again once the reset is triggered. Therefore, to justly evaluate the performance, we have computed RMSE in one of the time ranges where the system has reached one of the multiple steady states, between two resets. The RMSE of the estimate is of 128.62 mV for the DREM-LS without reset against the 27.34 mV of the DREM-LS with reset or the 514.15 mV of the KF in the steady state between time 3400 s - 4100 s, see Table 4 for comparison. The gains of the observers can be found in Table 8 for the τ observer and in Table 9 for the parameter and states observer.

The reset mechanism is employed to restore the alertness of the parameter observer and is applied exclusively to the IE profile, which is repeated across the entire SoC range. Note that it is not applied to the estimator of τ . This two key questions remain to be answered: how the reset is implemented and under which conditions it is triggered.

The observer architecture incorporating a reset mechanism follows the approach presented in [34], with necessary modifications. The first modification involves translating the system to continuous time; however, as noted in the reference, this transition is straightforward and is therefore not reproduced here.

The second modification resets the elements ξ and Ψ from Eqs. (6) and (9) to their initial conditions. The third and most complex modification concerns the integrator of
θ
ˆ
. Upon reset, the component corresponding to OCV is initialized not with its prior value but with an estimate obtained through Coulomb counting. It is important to recall that for validation, the OCV-SoC relationship was experimentally determined and introduced as a look-up table, therefore there may be some discrepancies between the look-up table OCV and the DREM-LS OCV.

This section addresses the criteria for triggering the reset. The reset is required when the observer loses its alertness, which is indicated by the convergence of Δ in (20) to a value of 1. However, performing a reset in the absence of excitation would be ineffective. Since excitation is primarily driven by the battery current, we ensure that sufficient excitation is present before triggering a reset.

To achieve this, we filter the derivative of the battery current using the following equation:
(25)
w
̇
= − γ w +
|
i
̇
b a t
|
where
γ > 0
. This formulation accumulates variations in
i
̇
b a t
while gradually forgetting past values. The reset is enabled when Δ approaches 1 and w exceeds a predefined threshold. In our case, we set
γ = 0 . 1
and the threshold to 3.

Fig. 19 illustrates the conditions under which the reset occurs. The top panel shows the estimated OCV against the reference obtained through Coulomb counting (left axis) and the battery current (right axis). The second panel displays on the left axis the evolution of Δ from (20), highlighting a scenario where its value has reached 1, indicating a loss of observer alertness. On the right axis, the value of w from (25) is depicted.

As observed, when the current remains constant, excitation is absent, making a reset ineffective. However, once current variations occur, w begins to increase. When it surpasses the threshold (set to 3), the reset is triggered. At this moment, the variables described in Section 5.5.1 are reset, and the OCV estimate is coupled with the Coulomb counting result. This effect is visible in the estimated OCV curve at the reset instant, where it aligns with the Coulomb counter value.

This study applies a novel observer architecture for estimating the OCV and related parameters in LIB and VRFB. The proposed approach introduces several key contributions: the relaxation of the PE condition to interval excitation, inherent to the architecture introduced in [34], a transformation of first-order ECM into linear regression problems, and the utilization of DREM-based least squares estimation. These innovations collectively enable accurate and robust OCV estimation even under challenging conditions.

The methodology was validated through a comprehensive set of simulations and experiments. Numerical simulations tested the estimator under diverse current profiles, including PE, IE, and the WLTP driving cycle, both in noiseless and noisy scenarios. Results demonstrated the estimator’s ability to provide precise OCV and voltage reconstructions, with acceptable error, even in the presence of noise. Experimental validations on LIB and VRFB systems further affirmed the effectiveness of the proposed architecture, showcasing its real-world applicability.

Compared to classical techniques such as KF and EKF, the proposed method proved to be more robust, particularly in scenarios involving IE profiles and noisy data. While Kalman-based methods struggled with observability limitations, the DREM-based approach excelled by effectively estimating time-varying parameters and reconstructing states.

The study underscores the practical advantages of the proposed plug & play framework, which minimizes the need for extensive prior knowledge of battery parameters, significantly enhancing its industrial viability.

Miquel Martí-Florences: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Andreu Cecilia: Writing – review & editing, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Romeo Ortega: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis. Alexey Bobtsov: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis. Ramon Costa-Castelló: Writing – review & editing, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix B
ce:section.ce:para: In this appendix, Table 4 is provided, with the RMSE computed in the last 85% of the cycle, as we believe is representative of the steady state in all cases. On the other hand, the Simulink main configuration parameters are given in Table 6. The tables with the tuning parameters for each of the two observers are also provided, for all the test cases. To ease the reading, Table 5 serves as a supplementary table linking the test cases, the τ observer (Tables 7, 8 and 10) and the parameters and states observer (Tables 9 and 11). The parameters of observers have been selected starting from the examples in [34] and manually tuning until a viable result is produced. Particularly in the PE and IE profiles, the variation of the parameters was not of much significance. In the WLTP case, however, the tuning of λ and α was more relevant, and differed between the simulated and the experimental cases.
